papers.title: The genetic signature of memory encoding along the human hippocampal axis.
papers.abstract: Episodic memory formation engages hippocampal oscillations that vary along the anterior-posterior axis, but the molecular programs supporting this physiological specialization remain unclear. Here, we leveraged a rare neurosurgical dataset in which patients performed verbal episodic memory tasks during intrahippocampal intracranial EEG recordings prior to en bloc hippocampal resection, enabling integration of encoding-related oscillatory signatures with matched cell-type-resolved transcriptomics from the same individuals. Subsequent memory effects (SMEs) spanned delta/theta, gamma, and hippoca
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study combined intracranial EEG recordings with cell-type-resolved transcriptomics from the same patients to investigate the molecular basis of memory encoding along the hippocampal axis. The authors identified distinct oscillatory signatures and gene expression programs associated with successful memory formation, linking specific cell types and molecular pathways to anterior-posterior hippocampal specialization.

papers.title: SimMapNet: a Bayesian framework for gene regulatory network inference using gene ontology similarities as external hint.
papers.abstract: Gene regulatory network (GRN) reconstruction is a fundamental challenge in computational biology, and is crucial for understanding gene interactions. In this study, we aim to incorporate Gene Ontology (GO) similarities into the construction of GRNs. Our key assumption is that genes with higher similarity in Molecular Function, Biological Process, or Cellular Component categories are more likely to be functionally related and, therefore, more likely to be connected in the network. We introduce SimMapNet, a Bayesian framework that estimates the precision matrix, which serves as the adjacency mat
papers.summary: SimMapNet is a new Bayesian method that improves gene regulatory network inference by incorporating Gene Ontology similarity as prior knowledge, under the assumption that functionally similar genes are more likely to be connected. The framework estimates a precision matrix to represent network adjacency, offering a principled way to integrate external biological information into network reconstruction.

papers.title: D-SPIN constructs regulatory network models from scRNA-seq that reveal organizing principles of perturbation response.
papers.abstract: Gene regulatory networks modulate the expression of the genome in response to signals and environmental conditions. Reconstructions of such networks can reveal the control principles cells use to maintain homeostasis and execute cell-state transitions. Here, we introduce a computational framework, dimension-scalable single-cell perturbation integration network (D-SPIN), that infers mechanistically interpretable and generative models of gene regulatory networks from single-cell mRNA-seq datasets collected across thousands of perturbation conditions. The models explain how perturbations modulate
papers.summary: D-SPIN is a new computational framework that infers interpretable gene regulatory network models from single-cell RNA-seq data collected across thousands of perturbation conditions. It reveals how perturbations modulate gene expression and the organizing principles cells use to maintain homeostasis and execute state transitions.

papers.title: Deep dynamical models of single-cell multiomic velocities predict loss-of-function and rescue perturbations in B cells.
papers.abstract: We present DynaVelo, a generative neural ordinary differential equation model that learns the joint dynamics of gene expression and transcription factor (TF) motif activities in evolving cell systems using single-cell multiome with joint gene expression and chromatin accesibility readout. DynaVelo leverages partial RNA velocity information together with single-cell TF motif accessibility data to improve the modeling of cell state dynamics and identification of TF drivers. We show that DynaVelo recovers the complex and bifurcating in vivo dynamics of wildtype murine germinal center (GC) B cells
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: DynaVelo is a new deep learning model that uses single-cell multiomic data to jointly model gene expression and transcription factor activity dynamics, improving the prediction of cell state transitions. Applied to mouse B cells, it accurately recovers complex developmental trajectories and can simulate the effects of genetic perturbations.

papers.title: pyVIPER: a fast and scalable Python package for protein activity estimation and master regulator analysis of single-cell RNA sequencing data.
papers.abstract: Single-cell sequencing has revolutionized biomedical research by offering insights into cellular heterogeneity at unprecedented resolution. Yet, the low signal-to-noise ratio characteristic of single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) challenges quantitative analyses. Gene regulatory network (GRN) analysis can help overcome this obstacle, enabling the mechanistic elucidation of cellular state determinants. For instance, the VIPER algorithm can identify Master Regulator proteins from gene expression data. However, as the size and complexity of scRNA-seq datasets grow, the demand for scalable tools
papers.summary: pyVIPER is a new Python package that enables fast and scalable estimation of protein activity and identification of master regulator proteins from single-cell RNA sequencing data. It addresses the computational challenges posed by large scRNA-seq datasets by providing an efficient implementation of the VIPER algorithm, which helps overcome the low signal-to-noise ratio inherent in single-cell data.

papers.title: A causal reinforcement learning framework for reliable gene regulatory network inference.
papers.abstract: Gene regulatory network inference is crucial for revealing the mechanisms of cellular functions and the causal mechanisms of disease occurrence. However, traditional correlation-based methods struggle to identify causal directions, and deep learning methods lack structural interpretability. Moreover, neither of them can efficiently adapt to the search of the solution space for large-scale gene regulatory networks, making it difficult to accurately capture the real regulatory patterns between genes. This study proposes a gene regulatory network inference method based on causal reinforcement lea
papers.summary: This paper introduces a new method for inferring gene regulatory networks that uses causal reinforcement learning to overcome limitations of traditional correlation-based and deep learning approaches. The method improves the identification of causal directions and structural interpretability, and efficiently handles large-scale networks. This represents a methodological advance in network inference.

papers.title: WNT signaling in human pluripotent stem cells promotes HDAC2-dependent epigenetic programs and development of retinoic acid-responsive mesoderm.
papers.abstract: Human pluripotent stem cells (hPSCs) can be used as a scalable source of lymphocytes for adoptive cell therapies, contingent on the robust generation of definitive hematopoietic intermediates. Early WNT activation with CHIR99021 during mesoderm induction promoted the formation of KDR+ ALDH1A2+ mesoderm, which is essential for subsequent generation of T cells in a retinoic acid (RA)-dependent manner. Integrated scRNA-seq and ATAC-seq defined a WNT-dependent developmental trajectory from hPSCs to KDR+ ALDH1A2+ mesoderm. Gene regulatory network modeling predicted HDAC2 and E-box transcription fac
papers.summary: This study shows that early WNT signaling activation in human pluripotent stem cells drives the formation of a specific mesoderm population (KDR+ ALDH1A2+) that is critical for generating T cells in a retinoic acid-dependent manner. Using single-cell RNA-seq and ATAC-seq, the authors mapped the developmental trajectory and identified HDAC2 and E-box transcription factors as key regulators of this WNT-dependent epigenetic program.

papers.title: RegVelo: Gene-regulatory-informed dynamics of single cells.
papers.abstract: Cell fate transitions are driven by regulatory circuitry, yet RNA velocity models cellular dynamics without explicitly accounting for gene regulatory interactions, limiting mechanistic insight. Conversely, gene regulatory network (GRN) inference methods largely neglect the dynamic nature of biological systems. To overcome this conceptual disconnect, we present RegVelo, a bottom-up, actionable, and interpretable deep learning framework that jointly models splicing kinetics and gene regulatory interactions. Across diverse biological systems, RegVelo provides reliable predictive power for termina
papers.summary: RegVelo is a new deep learning framework that integrates gene regulatory network information with RNA velocity models to better predict cell fate transitions. By jointly modeling splicing kinetics and regulatory interactions, it provides more accurate and interpretable predictions of cellular dynamics across diverse biological systems.

papers.title: PXDN, TCF4 and TSPAN7 Are Differentially Expressed in B-Cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia: An Integrative Analysis.
papers.abstract: Background/Objectives: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL) is a biologically heterogeneous disease in which transcriptional dysregulation contributes to disease onset and progression. Despite survival rates exceeding 90% in high-income countries, relapsed and high-risk cases remain a major clinical challenge, highlighting the need for improved molecular stratification, namely the classification of patients based on genetic and transcriptomic features associated with prognosis, therapeutic response, and disease biology, as well as for the identification of novel therapeutic targets. Methods: We
papers.summary: This study identifies three genes (PXDN, TCF4, and TSPAN7) that are differentially expressed in B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, suggesting they may serve as biomarkers or therapeutic targets. The findings highlight potential molecular candidates for improving risk stratification and treatment of high-risk and relapsed cases.

papers.title: Perturbation of genes linked to common schizophrenia risk variants identifies cilia programs.
papers.abstract: Schizophrenia (SCZ) is a common psychiatric disorder characterized by psychosis, emotional withdrawal, and cognitive deficits. Most SCZ risk variants reside in non-coding regions of the genome and are thought to influence disease risk by modulating gene regulation. However, the target genes, biological pathways, and cell types through which these variants exert their effects remain poorly understood. To address this gap, we employed in vivo CRISPR droplet sequencing (CROP-seq) in the postnatal mouse neocortex. We perturbed 12 SCZ risk genes previously linked to functionally validated risk vari
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study uses in vivo CRISPR droplet sequencing (CROP-seq) in the mouse neocortex to perturb 12 schizophrenia risk genes linked to non-coding risk variants. The findings reveal that these genes converge on cilia-related programs, suggesting a new biological pathway for schizophrenia risk.

papers.title: Causal intervention validation of gene regulatory signals in scGPT.
papers.abstract: Single-cell foundation models such as scGPT have been promoted as representations of gene regulation, but their advertised regulatory signal has been judged largely from attention weights, which are correlational. We ask a deliberately bounded question: whether direct interventions on scGPT gene tokens-causal with respect to the model's own computation-recover model-internal transcription-factor (TF)-target dependencies that align with curated references, whether that signal is robust, and whether it transfers to real perturbation responses. We separate these into two explicit validation axes
papers.summary: This study tests whether the single-cell foundation model scGPT captures genuine regulatory signals by performing causal interventions on its gene tokens, rather than relying on correlational attention weights. The authors find that model-internal transcription factor-target dependencies recovered through these interventions align with curated references and transfer to real perturbation responses. This provides a more rigorous validation of regulatory signal in scGPT beyond simple correlation.

papers.title: Spatial Transcriptomics in Ovarian Biology Technologies, Computational Challenges, and Biological Insights.
papers.abstract: The ovary is a structurally complex organ whose function depends on precisely coordinated interactions among multiple cell types. Spatially resolved transcriptomics (ST) has emerged as a powerful complement to single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), enabling gene expression profiling within intact tissue and preserving the spatial context that dissociation-based methods inherently lack. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the major ST platforms, including sequencing-based technologies (Visium, Visium HD, Stereo-seq, and GeoMx) and imaging-based technologies (Xenium, MERSCOPE, and
papers.summary: This review discusses how spatially resolved transcriptomics (ST) technologies are being applied to study the ovary, a complex organ where cell interactions are critical. It covers major ST platforms and their potential to provide new biological insights into ovarian function by preserving spatial context that single-cell RNA-seq loses.

papers.title: Single-cell multi-omic atlas and morphogen screening informs midbrain and hindbrain organoid engineering.
papers.abstract: Patterning of the neural tube establishes midbrain and hindbrain structures that coordinate motor movement, process sensory input and integrate cognitive functions. Cellular impairment within these structures underlies diverse neurological disorders, and in vitro organoid models promise inroads to understanding development and modeling disease. Here, we use paired single-cell transcriptome and accessible chromatin sequencing to map cell composition and regulatory mechanisms in organoid models of midbrain and hindbrain. We find that existing midbrain organoid protocols generate ventral and dors
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell multi-omic sequencing to map cell types and regulatory mechanisms in midbrain and hindbrain organoids, revealing that current protocols predominantly produce ventral cell types. The findings provide a resource for improving organoid engineering to better model neurological disorders.

papers.title: Ensemble threshold Boolean modeling reveals robust attractors and regulatory drivers in pediatric leukemia.
papers.abstract: Pediatric B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL) is characterized by substantial phenotypic heterogeneity, reflecting the complex structure of its underlying gene regulatory network (GRN). To investigate the dynamical principles governing these phenotypes, we model a curated GRN as a threshold Boolean network (TBN) and analyze its behavior across a large ensemble of weighted network realizations sharing the same topology. For each realization, we compute all asymptotic states and project them onto leukemia-relevant key genes to obtain a reduced attractor representation. The ensemble revea
papers.summary: This study models the gene regulatory network of pediatric B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia using an ensemble of threshold Boolean networks. The analysis reveals robust attractors and identifies key regulatory drivers, providing insights into the dynamical principles underlying the disease's phenotypic heterogeneity.
